items.subject: neuroscience
items.topic: how mirror neurons relate to empathy and imitation
items.transcript: Mirror neurons are one of the most talked-about discoveries in modern neuroscience, and today I want to explain what they are and why so many people find them fascinating. Back in the early nineteen-nineties, a team in Parma, Italy, was recording from single neurons in a monkey's brain, in a region involved in planning movement. They noticed something strange. A particular neuron fired when the monkey reached for a peanut, and that part made sense. But the same neuron also fired when the monkey simply watched a researcher reach for the peanut. The monkey wasn't moving at all. So this one cell responded both to doing an action and to seeing that same action performed by someone else. That's why they're called mirror neurons: the brain seems to mirror what it observes. Now, why does this matter? One idea is that mirror neurons help us learn by imitation. When you watch someone tie a knot or swing a tennis racket, your motor system quietly rehearses the same movement, which may make copying it easier. A second, bolder proposal connects them to empathy. The thinking goes that when you see someone stub their toe and wince, the circuits that would be active if it happened to you switch on, so you don't just observe their pain, you share it a little. I should add a note of caution, though. Much of the strongest evidence still comes from monkeys, and measuring individual neurons in humans is genuinely difficult. So while mirror neurons offer a tempting bridge between action, imitation, and feeling, researchers continue to debate exactly how much they really explain.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: What mirror neurons are and why researchers find them significant
options.B: The methods used to record electrical activity in monkey brains
options.C: How people learn to play tennis by copying an instructor
options.D: The differences between empathy and imitation in humans

questions.explanation: 讲座先解释镜像神经元是什么，再谈它们为何重要（模仿与共情），A 概括了整体主旨，正确。B 只讲了帕尔马实验的记录方法，是细节，too_narrow。C 借用讲座里"打网球"这个例子，但那只是模仿学习的举例，不是主旨，surface_word_trap。D 讲座并没有比较共情与模仿的"区别"，而是把两者都作为镜像神经元的可能功能，wrong_topic。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe the experiment with the monkey and the peanut?

options.A: To show that monkeys can be trained to perform complex tasks
options.B: To illustrate how one neuron responded both to acting and to watching
options.C: To point out that the neuron fired when the monkey grabbed the peanut
options.D: To argue that monkey and human brains work in identical ways

questions.explanation: 教授讲这个实验，是为了说明同一个神经元既在"做动作"时放电，也在"看别人做"时放电，这正是镜像神经元的定义，B 正确。A 误判目的，讲座重点不是猴子能被训练，wrong_purpose。C 只是复述"神经元在猴子抓花生时放电"这一事实，没有解释"为什么提"，literal_restatement。D 把讲座没有表达的观点强加进去，教授并未断言猴脑与人脑完全相同，反而后面提醒人类证据有限，over_inference。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention watching someone tie a knot or swing a tennis racket?

options.A: To suggest that these particular skills are unusually hard to master
options.B: To prove that mirror neurons control all of human movement
options.C: To give an everyday example of how mirror neurons may aid imitation
options.D: To describe the exact movements the motor system performs

questions.explanation: 这两个日常场景是用来说明镜像神经元如何帮助我们通过"模仿学习"，C 正确。A 误判目的，教授并非在强调这些技能有多难，wrong_purpose。B 夸大成"控制全部人类运动"，讲座没有这么说，over_inference。D 只是复述动作本身，而没有解释"为什么举这个例子"，literal_restatement。

questions.stem: Why does the professor add a note of caution near the end of the lecture?

options.A: To claim that mirror neurons completely explain human empathy
options.B: To restate that the neurons were first found in a monkey's brain
options.C: To suggest that studying monkeys has no real scientific value
options.D: To point out that the evidence is limited and the claims still debated

questions.explanation: 结尾的"提醒"是为了指出：多数证据来自猴子、人类测量困难，因此这些说法仍有争议，D 正确。A 与教授意思相反，他恰恰在收紧而非放大主张，over_inference。B 只是复述"在猴脑中发现"这一事实，不是提醒的目的，literal_restatement。C 误判目的，教授并没有否定研究猴子的价值，wrong_purpose。

items.subject: physics
items.topic: how light refraction produces a rainbow
items.transcript: Rainbows are such a familiar sight that we rarely stop to ask how they actually form. The answer lies in what happens when sunlight passes through a raindrop. Light travels at different speeds in air and in water, and when a beam crosses from one into the other, it bends. We call this bending refraction. Here's the key point: sunlight looks white, but it's really a mixture of many colors, and each color bends by a slightly different amount. Red light bends the least, and violet light bends the most. So when white light enters a spherical raindrop, it refracts, spreads out into a fan of colors, reflects off the back of the drop, and then refracts again as it exits. That double bending separates the colors and sends them back toward your eyes at slightly different angles. Now, an important detail. You can only see a rainbow when the sun is behind you and the rain is in front of you. Each raindrop sends just one color to your eye, depending on its position in the sky, and millions of drops together produce the full arc that you see. Red appears along the outer edge and violet along the inner edge. And because the geometry depends entirely on where you're standing, no two people ever see exactly the same rainbow. The person next to you is actually looking at light from a different set of drops. So a rainbow isn't really an object sitting out there in the sky. It's a pattern of light that exists only from your particular point of view, built fresh for each observer.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: The different speeds at which light moves through air
options.B: How refraction inside raindrops produces the colors of a rainbow
options.C: Why the daytime sky appears blue on a clear day
options.D: How raindrops first form inside storm clouds

questions.explanation: 整场讲座解释阳光进入雨滴后如何折射、分光、反射再折射，最终形成彩虹，B 概括主旨，正确。A 只是解释折射原理时的一个前提细节，too_narrow。C 天空为何是蓝的属于相关但不同的话题，讲座没讲，wrong_topic。D 借用"雨滴/雨"这个词，但"雨如何形成"不是本讲内容，surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, which color of light bends the most?

options.A: Red light, which bends more sharply than violet does
options.B: Every color bends by exactly the same amount in water
options.C: Violet light, which bends the most of all the colors
options.D: Blue light, because water absorbs it most strongly

questions.explanation: 教授明确说红光弯折最少、紫光弯折最多，C 正确。A 把关系颠倒了，讲座说红光弯折最少，reversed_logic。B 与讲座直接矛盾，讲座强调各色弯折程度不同，wrong_detail。D 提到"吸收"这一讲座未涉及的机制，属该领域看似合理但未陈述的内容，topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: Why does the professor say the sun must be behind you to see a rainbow?

options.A: To prove that rainbows can only appear early in the morning
options.B: To restate that light reflects off the back of the raindrop
options.C: To explain why violet ends up on the outer edge of the arc
options.D: To describe the specific viewing geometry a rainbow requires

questions.explanation: 这句话是为了说明看到彩虹需要特定的几何位置关系（太阳在后、雨在前），D 正确。A 夸大推断，讲座没说彩虹只在清晨出现，over_inference。B 只是复述"光在雨滴背面反射"这一事实，不是这句话的目的，literal_restatement。C 误判目的，且与事实相反（紫在内、红在外），wrong_purpose。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about two people standing side by side?

options.A: Each of them sees a rainbow made from a different set of drops
options.B: They see identical rainbows because the sunlight is the same
options.C: Neither of them can see a rainbow at the same moment
options.D: One of them sees only red while the other sees only violet

questions.explanation: 教授说彩虹取决于观察者位置，旁边的人其实在看另一组雨滴的光，故每人看到的彩虹来自不同雨滴，A 正确。B 与讲座相反，讲座强调没有两人看到完全相同的彩虹，reversed_logic。C 过度推断，讲座并未说两人无法同时看到彩虹，over_generalization。D 是该话题下看似相关却无依据的臆测，讲座没说一人只见红、另一人只见紫，topic_adjacent。

items.subject: psychology
items.topic: the bystander effect in emergency situations
items.transcript: Imagine someone collapses on a busy city sidewalk. You'd think that the more people who are around, the more likely that person is to get help. But research in social psychology suggests almost the opposite. This is known as the bystander effect: the presence of other people can actually make any single individual less likely to step in and help. The classic explanation involves two mechanisms. The first is diffusion of responsibility. When you're the only witness, all the responsibility to act falls on you. But when there are twenty witnesses, that responsibility feels spread out. Everyone assumes someone else will handle it, so no one does. The second mechanism is more subtle. It's called pluralistic ignorance. In an ambiguous situation, people look to others to decide whether something is really an emergency. If everyone around you appears calm, you may conclude there's no real problem, even while each of those calm people is quietly reaching the same mistaken conclusion by watching you. In one well-known experiment, participants sat filling out a questionnaire when smoke began seeping into the room. Alone, most people reported the smoke quickly. But when seated with others who deliberately ignored it, the majority stayed put, sometimes waving the smoke away and saying nothing. Now, here's what I find important. The bystander effect isn't really about people being cruel or apathetic. It's about how social cues and shared responsibility quietly reshape individual behavior. And simply knowing about the effect can help. If you understand why you might hesitate, you're more likely to override that hesitation and actually act.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why large cities tend to be more dangerous than rural areas
options.B: How to correctly perform first aid during a medical emergency
options.C: Why people are less likely to help when others are present
options.D: How questionnaires are designed for psychology experiments

questions.explanation: 讲座核心是旁观者效应，即他人在场反而降低个体伸出援手的可能，C 正确。A 城市是否更危险不是讲座话题，wrong_topic。B 借"急救/紧急"语义设陷阱，讲座并不教如何急救，surface_word_trap。D 问卷只是烟雾实验里的道具细节，不是主旨，too_narrow。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what is diffusion of responsibility?

options.A: The tendency to help faster when a large crowd is watching
options.B: A method researchers use to measure emergency response times
options.C: The wave of panic that spreads quickly through a large crowd
options.D: The sense that the duty to act is shared among many witnesses

questions.explanation: 教授定义责任分散为：目击者众多时，行动责任被分摊，人人以为别人会处理，D 正确。A 与讲座相反，讲座说人多时反而更慢/更不愿帮，reversed_logic。B 把它误当成一种测量方法，属看似合理却无依据，topic_adjacent。C 偷换概念，责任分散不是"恐慌蔓延"，wrong_detail。

questions.stem: What happened in the experiment when smoke entered the room?

options.A: People seated with unconcerned others usually stayed and said nothing
options.B: Participants left the room faster whenever others were also present
options.C: Everyone reported the smoke right away no matter who was there
options.D: The smoke turned out to be part of a scheduled fire drill

questions.explanation: 实验发现：与故意无动于衷的人同坐时，多数被试留在原地、什么都不说，A 正确。B 关系颠倒，讲座说有他人在场反而更少行动，reversed_logic。C 与讲座矛盾，单独一人时才大多立刻报告，wrong_detail。D 凭空添加"消防演习"情节，讲座未提，topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the bystander effect?

options.A: It proves that most people are fundamentally cold and uncaring
options.B: Being aware of it can make a person more likely to help
options.C: It disappears completely once a crowd grows large enough
options.D: It happens only in laboratory settings, never in real life

questions.explanation: 教授结尾指出，了解这一效应有助于人克服犹豫、主动行动，B 正确。A 过度概括，教授明说这不是因为人冷漠，over_generalization。C 与讲座相反，人越多效应越强而非消失，reversed_logic。D 无依据的臆断，讲座以街头场景开篇，说明现实中也存在，topic_adjacent。

items.subject: biology
items.transcript: Last week we looked at how some desert plants survive long droughts. Today I want to turn to an animal that takes survival to an even greater extreme: the tardigrade. Tardigrades, sometimes called water bears, are tiny creatures, usually less than a millimeter long, that live in moss, soil, and water all over the world. What makes them remarkable is their ability to endure conditions that would kill almost anything else. They've survived being frozen to near absolute zero, heated past the boiling point of water, and even exposed to the vacuum of outer space. So how do they do it? The secret is a process called cryptobiosis. When its environment dries out, a tardigrade pulls in its legs, expels most of the water from its body, and curls into a tiny, dehydrated ball called a tun. In this state, its metabolism nearly stops. It's essentially paused, showing almost no signs of life. To protect itself, the tardigrade produces special sugars and proteins that replace the lost water and shield its cells from damage. A tun can remain in this suspended condition for years. Then, when water returns, the tardigrade slowly rehydrates, and within hours it's walking around again as if nothing had happened. Now, why should we care about a millimeter-long animal? Well, scientists studying these protective proteins hope to use them to preserve things like vaccines and delicate cells without refrigeration. So this humble creature may end up teaching us how to keep fragile biological materials alive far longer than we can manage today.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: The desert plants that survive long periods of drought
options.B: How hospitals store vaccines inside cold refrigerators
options.C: The various places where tiny water bears can be found
options.D: How tardigrades survive extreme conditions and why it matters

questions.explanation: 讲座主体讲缓步动物如何靠隐生度过极端环境，以及研究它的意义，D 正确。A 是上周内容、今天只作引子，wrong_topic。B 借"疫苗/冷藏"词，但讲座讲的是用蛋白替代冷藏，而非医院如何冷藏，surface_word_trap。C 栖息地只是开头一句细节，too_narrow。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what does a tardigrade do during cryptobiosis?

options.A: It expels most of its water and curls into a ball called a tun
options.B: It speeds up its metabolism to fight against the cold
options.C: It grows a hard outer shell to protect its soft body
options.D: It lays eggs that are able to survive without any water

questions.explanation: 教授说隐生时缓步动物排出体内大部分水分、蜷成叫做 tun 的脱水小球，A 正确。B 关系颠倒，讲座说其代谢几乎停止而非加快，reversed_logic。C 偷换事实，讲座讲的是产生糖和蛋白保护细胞，不是长硬壳，wrong_detail。D 凭空添加"产卵"，讲座未提，topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: According to the professor, how does a tardigrade protect its cells when dried out?

options.A: By keeping its body fully hydrated at all times
options.B: By producing special sugars and proteins that replace lost water
options.C: By lowering its temperature down to near absolute zero
options.D: By burrowing deeper into the soil to find hidden moisture

questions.explanation: 教授明确说缓步动物产生特殊的糖和蛋白，替代流失的水分并保护细胞，B 正确。A 与讲座相反，隐生恰恰是排出水分而非保持水润，reversed_logic。C 把它能"耐受"近绝对零度错当成它"主动降温"的保护手段，wrong_detail。D 讲座没提钻入土中找水这一行为，topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about studying tardigrades?

options.A: Their proteins will soon replace refrigerators in every hospital
options.B: Their protective proteins are too fragile to be scientifically useful
options.C: Their survival strategies could help preserve biological materials
options.D: Only their frozen state is of any real interest to researchers

questions.explanation: 教授暗示研究这些保护性蛋白可帮助无需冷藏地保存疫苗和细胞，即帮助保存生物材料，C 正确。A 夸大成"很快取代所有医院冰箱"，over_generalization。B 与讲座相反，教授看好这些蛋白的应用价值，reversed_logic。D 无依据缩窄，讲座关注的是隐生保护机制，而非只关注冷冻状态，topic_adjacent。
